title: The Baptism of Our Lord
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
January 10, 2021 | "The Baptism of Our Lord" | Preacher: Katie Pearson

## 1. LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The First Sunday after Epiphany, observed as the Feast of the Baptism of Our Lord (also called the Baptism of Christ), falling within the Christmas/Epiphany season. The RCL readings for this date would be Isaiah 42:1-9, Psalm 29, Acts 10:34-43, and Matthew 3:13-17, focusing on Christ's baptism in the Jordan River and the voice from heaven declaring Jesus as God's beloved Son. This celebration marks the liturgical commemoration of Jesus's baptism by John the Baptist and emphasizes themes of divine affirmation, calling, and mission at the threshold between Epiphany and Ordinary Time.

## 2. EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in the immediate aftermath of the January 6, 2021 Capitol riot, which had deeply fractured national discourse and created significant tension within Anglican/Episcopal congregations about Christian witness and political violence. Episcopal leadership at all levels was issuing statements and responses to the insurrection. The Diocese of Colorado and the broader church were grappling with how to address the involvement of some self-identified Christians in the events of January 6th. This would have been a crucial moment for preaching about baptismal identity and Christian calling in the face of national fracture.

## 3. DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was under winter weather conditions typical for early January, with the city still processing the shock of the January 6th Capitol riot and its Colorado connections—several Coloradans were identified among those who breached the Capitol. The Capitol Hill neighborhood, directly adjacent to downtown where Saint John's Cathedral is located, remained a focal point of civic attention and concern. Local police and community leaders were engaged in addressing both the national political crisis and its local manifestations.

## 4. NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The nation was in immediate crisis response following the January 6 insurrection at the U.S. Capitol, with ongoing investigations, arrests, and profound questions about domestic extremism and Christian nationalism. President Trump faced a second impeachment (announced January 10 itself, proceeding through the House). The COVID-19 pandemic was accelerating with over 3,700 deaths reported on January 9 alone, and vaccine distribution was beginning but progressing unevenly across states. The 2020 election certification had been violently contested just four days prior, and democratic institutions were under unprecedented scrutiny.

## 5. INTERNATIONAL NEWS

Brazil was experiencing a devastating surge in COVID-19 cases with hospitals overwhelmed and a new variant (P.1) driving the surge in the Brazilian Amazon region, raising global concerns about viral mutation. India was beginning its massive vaccination campaign while simultaneously experiencing farmer protests against agricultural reform laws that had extended into their third month. Myanmar was on the verge of political crisis, with military tensions rising ahead of the February 1 coup that would occur weeks later.

## 6. CULTURAL MOMENT

The national zeitgeist on January 10, 2021 was characterized by trauma, disorientation, and a search for moral clarity following the violent assault on the Capitol. There was deep questioning within American religious communities about Christian identity and complicity. The mood was one of genuine constitutional crisis—many Americans felt the legitimacy of democratic institutions had been fundamentally threatened. Sports provided some normalcy, with NFL playoff games proceeding that weekend. The cultural moment was marked by urgency around "What does it mean to be Christian in America?" making the baptism sermon particularly loaded with contemporary relevance.
